coredata.dc:title: Chemical treatment of biomasswastes as carbon dot carriers for solar-driven water purification
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yifan
Chang, Qing
Xue, Chaorui
Yang, Jinlong
Hu, Shengliang
coredata.dc:description: A purely chemical method is demonstrated to treat a variety of biomass wastes for extracting cellulose nanofibrils (CNFs) with a consistent property. By hydrothermal reaction, carbon dots (CDs) can be easily grafted on the surface of CNFs to act as photo-thermal agents and enable fast water evaporation rate at 2.5 kg m−2h−1 with about 96.45% solar-to-vapor efficiency under one sun irradiation. This derives from good hydration ability of this system, which lowers the evaporation enthalpy. Moreover, this system not only adsorbs dye contaminants effectively by the formation of hydrogen bonds, but also possesses long-term antifouling solar desalination by means of rationally drilled millimeter-sized channels. Given the sustainable biomass resources and scalable fabrication process, this work offers a promising strategy towards construct low-cost evaporators with the excellent water purification performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass materials
Solar evaporation
Carbon dots
Water purification

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurement of solar water evaporation performance
Characterization of morphologies, structures and properties

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of preparing the evaporator of CNFs@CDs.

Vapor generation under 1 sun. (a) Mass change of vapor generation with the evaporators of CNFs@CDs derived from different biomass wastes over irradiation time; (b) Evaporation performance comparison between CNFs@CDs and CS1; (c) The evaporation performances of CNFs@CDs prepared by using different CDs concentrations; (d) The surface temperatures of CNFs@CDs prepared by using different CDs concentrations; (e) Cycle stabilities of CNFs@CDs derived from different biomass wastes; (f) The acid and alkali resistance of CNFs@CDs.
(a-c) SEM images of CNFs; (d-f) SEM images of CNFs@CDs; (g) XRD patterns of CNFs and CNFs@CDs; (h) XPS C 1 s spectra of CNFs and CNFs@CDs; (i) UV–Vis-NIR absorption spectra of CNFs and CNFs@CDs.

(a) MB adsorption of CNFs and CNFs@CDs under dark condition; (b) MB adsorption of CNFs and CNFs@CDs under one sun irradiation and dark condition, respectively; (c) The effect of initial MB concentrations on adsorption performance of CNFs@CDs under one sun irradiation; (d) Residual ratio of different MB concentrations using CNFs@CDs as sorbent under one sun.

(a) Mass change of vapor generation with CNFs@CDs in brine with different salinities under 1 sun; (b) ER of CNFs@CDs before and after drilling channels over time under 1 sun; (c) Surface temperatures of CNFs@CDs before and after drilling under 1 sun; (d) Self-regeneration properties of CNFs@CDs without drilling in brine; (e) Schematic illustration of enhanced convection with drilled channels for salt-rejection.

head.ce:title: Chemical treatment of biomasswastes as carbon dot carriers for solar-driven water purification

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A universal method was provided for constructing the solar-driven interfacial evaporation system with effective energy conversion and superior water hydration abilities using cellulose nanofibrils from different biomass wastes and carbon dots.

A purely chemical method is demonstrated to treat a variety of biomass wastes for extracting cellulose nanofibrils (CNFs) with a consistent property. By hydrothermal reaction, carbon dots (CDs) can be easily grafted on the surface of CNFs to act as photo-thermal agents and enable fast water evaporation rate at 2.5 kg m−2h−1 with about 96.45% solar-to-vapor efficiency under one sun irradiation. This derives from good hydration ability of this system, which lowers the evaporation enthalpy. Moreover, this system not only adsorbs dye contaminants effectively by the formation of hydrogen bonds, but also possesses long-term antifouling solar desalination by means of rationally drilled millimeter-sized channels. Given the sustainable biomass resources and scalable fabrication process, this work offers a promising strategy towards construct low-cost evaporators with the excellent water purification performance.

ce:section.ce:para: Increasing demand for clean water in industry, agriculture, energy, and municipal sectors, combined with limited natural freshwater, necessitates the rapid exploitation of technologies that can provide reliable access to clean and safe water from alternative sources [1–5]. Nontraditional water sources including of seawater and wastewater may supply abundant water globally [4], but these water sources suffer from high salt concentrations, various toxic contaminants and viruses. Nearly 30% of the population remains lack of safe drinking water so far [6], meanwhile unsafe drinking water gives rise to>1500,000 diarrheal deaths each year [7]. Accordingly, addressing the clean water scarcity is of paramount importance for human sustainable development.

Constructing rapid water evaporation systems utilizing abundant solar energy has been considered as a promising technology to separate water and salts/contaminants in solar distillation process [1–4]. Recently, a new technology for solar evaporation enhancement has come out where the emphasis is to avoid heat loss to bulk water and instead enable surface heating rapidly by localizing solar conversion heat at the water–air interface, enhancing the solar energy utilization beyond water production [3,8–11]. There have been various prototypes using bioinspired microstructures and nanomaterial/hybrid-based solar absorbers for surface heating, with solar steam conversion efficiencies of > 90% for these evaporators [3,8,9,12]. Nevertheless, at present, the raw materials constructing high-performance evaporators still confront cost and scalability barriers in the technology. Considering economically challenged populations, the renewable biomass/waste materials and more cost-effective solar absorbers could be desirable [13–15].

Cellulose nanofibrils (CNFs), which can be extracted by alkaline or acid cooking followed by a bleaching step, are renewable one-dimensional nanomaterials with naturally abundant pores and the hydrophilic nature [16,17]. As thus, CNFs can not only assist to facilitate macro-/meso-porous structures for efficient water transport but also endow good hydration ability to lower the evaporation enthalpy [18–20]. In combination with their excellent heat insulation property, CNFs could be ideal carriers of absorbers for enabling fast solar-converted heat transfer to water, good for water evaporation. However, the morphology and properties of CNFs highly depend on the chosen method and raw biomass resources [21]. To achieve consistent CNFs from different biomass resources, we developed a new chemical method to treat biomass wastes such as agricultural and forestry residues, crops and wood for production of qualified CNFs. By a simple hydrothermal reaction, carbon dots (CDs, a kind of fluorescent carbon-based quasi-spherical nanoparticles with sizes smaller than 10 nm [22]) were spatially grafted on the surface of these CNFs to enhance heat localization at the evaporation interface [23–25]. We found that CNFs derived from different biomass resources as carbon dot carriers showed a similar water evaporation performance with a high evaporation rate (ER) of around 2.5 kg m−2h−1 and solar-to-vapor efficiency of 96.45% under one sun irradiation. Meanwhile, the evaporator of CDs-loading CNFs (named as CNFs@CDs) also possessed strong adsorption to organic dyes and superior self-regenerating ability after drilling millimeter-sized channels, suggesting the great applications in the purification of nontraditional water sources.

Sugarcane bagasse, balsa wood, wheat husk and wheat straw were purchased from Alibaba (China) Network Technology Co., Ltd. (Hangzhou, China), respectively. Chemicals including Sodium hydroxide (>97%), sodium sulfite (>98%), hydrogen peroxide (>30%), formic acid (88%), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) with the average molecular weight of 15,000 were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), glutaraldehyde (50 wt% aqueous solution) and HCl (37%) were purchased from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). All the chemicals were used without further purification.

Typically, 25 g of biomass wastes such as sugarcane bagasse, balsa wood, wheat husk or wheat straw was treated in a mixed solution of NaOH (2 M) and Na2SO3 (0.095 M) at 90 °C for 5 h, followed by filtration to obtain the solid products. After washing with water, subsequently, the resultant products were further treated in the H2O2 solution (2 M) at 90 °C for 1 h [19]. By further filtration and washing, the solid products were immersed into the mixture solution of H2O2 (10 M, 15 mL) and formic acid (23.34 M, 150 mL) at room temperature for 24 h. Finally, the solid CNFs were gained by filtration and squeezing to remove excess solution.

CDs were fabricated from chemically selective oxidation of coal pitch as reported in our previous work[26]. Firstly, 200 mg of carbon dot powders was dispersed into 20 mL of ethanol by ultrasound, followed by adding 2 g of as-prepared solid CNFs mentioned above. Secondly, all of the prepared mixture was transferred into a 50 mL of reactor and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained suspension was filtrated to get CDs-loading CNFs. Thirdly, 200 mg of CDs-loading CNFs was dispersed into water again, and after that a circular shape sheet with a diameter of 15 mm and a thickness of around 2 mm was achieved through suction filtration. Fourthly, taking 100 μL of the mixed solution containing 0.65 g PVA, 125 μL glutaraldehyde and 10 mL DI water was coated on the above sheet by simple brush-painting method, followed by spraying 40 μL of HCl solution (1.5 M) on the surface. To perform gelation for 10 min, the sample was frozen in refrigerator (-18 °C) and then thawed in 25 °C water. After undergoing five times of freezing-thawing process, the evaporator of CNFs@CDs was gained finally. To compare, a control sample as the evaporator (Labeled as pure CNFs) was prepared using the processes as those for CNFs@CDs except for without adding CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of solar water evaporation performance
ce:section.ce:para: The water evaporation experiments were performed using a solar simulator (CEL-HXUV300, CEAULIGHT, China) outputting simulated solar flux at 1000 W•m−2 (one sun). The solar flux was confirmed using an optical power meter (CEL-NP2000, CEAuLight). A sample was floated on pure water (or brine for desalination tests) in a cylinder container that was surrounded by thermal insulating polystyrene foam. The mass of the water loss was measured by an electronic balance with 0.1 μg resolution. The surface temperature and the temperature of the bottom water region were monitored using a dual-channel thermocouple. During the evaporation experiments, the relative humidity and temperature in surroundings kept at around 40% and 25 °C, respectively.

The efficiency (η) for solar-vapor conversion was estimated by[20,27].
(1)
η =
m ̇
H e
C
opt
q 0

where ṁ, C opt, q 0 are the ER, the optical concentration, the solar flux intensity of one sun, respectively. He represents the equivalent evaporation enthalpy of the water in evaporator, which can be gained by evaporating water in dark chamber based on the following equation reported by Yu group[20,27],
(2)
H e
=
H 0
m 0
/
m e
H0 and m0 represents the evaporation enthalpy and ER of bulk water without solar irradiation, respectively; me refers to the ER of the sample evaporator at the same condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of morphologies, structures and properties
ce:section.ce:para:
Optical absorption of samples was conducted on a UV–VIS-NIR spectrophotometer (Hitachi U4100) with an integrated sphere in the range of 250–2,500 nm wavelengths. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) observation was taken with a Hitachi S-4800 microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data of all samples was collected by a Kratos AXIS 165 mutitechnique electron spectrometer having an Al Ka X-ray source for determining the composition and chemical bonding configurations. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) analysis was performed with DSC Q2000 (TA, USA). Dye adsorption experiments were conducted in a beaker containing dye solution with the known concentrations. Dye concentration was determined by monitoring the characteristic absorbance peak using a UV–visible spectrophotometer. The amount of dye adsorbed on per unit of adsorbent (q) at the sampling time was calculated by the following equations[28]:
(3)
q =
C 0
- C
m
× V

where C 0 is the initial dye concentration (mg•L-1), C represents the free dye concentration in the solution at the sampling time (mg•L-1), V is the volume of dye solution (mL) and m is the weight of adsorbent (g) used.

As illustrated in Fig. 1 , four typical biomass wastes including sugarcane bagasse, balsa wood, wheat husk and wheat straw were chosen as raw materials of preparing CNFs. The raw materials were first cooked in a mixture solution of NaOH and Na2SO3 at 90 °C for 5 h, followed by boiling in the H2O2 solution at 90 °C for 1 h to further remove the residual lignin[19]. Then the obtained samples were treated in the mixed solution of H2O2 and formic acid (FA) at room temperature for 24 h to obtain CNFs. After delignification, the yellow samples totally turn white (Figure S1). To combine with CDs, CNFs were further dispersed in the suspension of CDs, followed by hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 6 h to get CDs-grafted CNFs. By suctionfiltration, the circular shape sheet of samples with a diameter of 15 mm were prepared and subsequently performed in situ gelation of poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) similar with our previously reported processes to obtain biomass-based evaporators[25]. The obtained evaporator of CNFs@CDs has good toughness (Figure S2).

The vapour-generation efficiency of CNFs@CDs was quantified by recording the weight loss of water as a function of time. From Fig. 2
a, without illumination, the CNFs@CDs enables water evaporation faster than only the water, but it remains very low in efficiency. With illumination, the cumulative weight loss increases linearly with irradiation time, and particularly all of CNFs-based evaporators derived from different biomass wastes show no clear difference in water evaporation performance. The ER of CNFs@CDs was calculated to be 2.5 kg m−2h−1 under one-sun irradiation. In the meantime, the surface temperature of CNFs@CDs also raise to 41.3 ℃ from 21.5 ℃ (Figure S3). By plenty of highly hydratable –OH groups on cellulose molecules, CNFs possess the superior ability to capture water molecules to form bound water through hydrogen bonding [16]. Therefore, this feature could endow CNFs@CDs with an excellent hydrability and a high proportion of intermediate water (IW), which locates in an intermediate region between bound water and free water. The IW has been proved as activated water [20], which enables the energy demand of water evaporation to be lowered efficiently. Water vaporization enthalpy in CNFs@CDs was measured by differential scanning calorimetry (DSC), verifying a significantly reduced vaporization enthalpy compared to that of pure bulk water as well (Figure S4). Although PVA gels are also used to mold the shape, their content is very low in CNFs@CDs. As thus, the reduction of vaporization enthalpy for CNFs@CDs could be mainly ascribed to the characteristic of CNFs. By using the calculated method reported by Yu group[20,27] (see Experimental details, Table S1), the energy efficiency (η) for solar-vapor conversion of CNFs@CDs reaches up to 96.45% under 1 sun illumination.

Without the treatment of sample in the mixed solution of H2O2 and FA, however, the obtained CDs-loaded CNFs (named as CS1) exhibits the significantly lower water evaporation compared with CNFs@CDs (Fig. 2 b). This demonstrates that further treatment by the mixed solution of H2O2 and FA is necessitated to facilitate the combination of CDs and CNFs. The effect of CDs concentrations on the evaporation performance of CNFs@CDs was also examined carefully. The color of CNFs@CDs changed from the white to the black as the CDs increased (Figure S5). Fig. 2 c gives that the evaporation performance of CNFs@CDs gradually increases before 10 mg•mL−1 of CDs concentration, but it will keep constant when more CDs are added. Under one-sun irradiation, the CNFs@CDs samples achieved from different concentrations of CDs exhibit distinct differences in surface equilibrium temperatures (Fig. 2 d). It can be found that the equilibrium temperature of the sample prepared from 10 mg•mL−1 of CDs concentration rises up to the highest value, whereas that from the higher CDs concentration presents a relatively lower value. As our previous work reported [26], excessive CDs could cause large agglomerations during depositing processes. These agglomerations generally have low thermal conductivity and prolong the heat transfer from the bulk to surface, so influencing the surface temperature. Afterward, the sample prepared with 10 mg•mL−1 concentration will be employed to further investigate water purification performance of CNFs@CDs except otherwise specified. Moreover, it can be found that the CNFs@CDs owns excellent stability and good tolerance toward harsh conditions. From Fig. 2 e and 2f, the ER remains unchanged when CNFs@CDs reuses over 6 cycles and works in the conditions of strong acid (1 M HCl) and strong alkali (1 M NaOH) for 12 h at least. The steam generation performances of the evaporator were also examined under different solar intensities (Figure S6), revealing the strong dependence of the evaporation performance with solar intensity as well as the excellent photostability.

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of CNFs (taking derived from sugarcane bagasse as an example) show two kinds of morphology features: fibers and slices, which result from cracked fibers (Fig. 3
a-c). By further magnification, the observed fibers present many holes and wrinkles (Fig. 3b and c). After loading CDs, porous structure of fibers still exists (Fig. 3 d-f). More importantly, the CDs and their aggregations have been observed on the surface of fibers based on magnified images (Fig. 3e and f). In addition, the crystalline structures of CNFs was examined by the X-ray diffraction (XRD) to gain further insights into the structural changes before and after loading CDs (Fig. 3 g). Due to the strong hydrogen bonding and van der Waals interactions, a diffraction peak at around 22.7° is present in pure CNFs, corresponding to (200) diffraction planes [17]. As for CNFs@CDs, this peak becomes stronger markedly owing to the introduction of CDs. The chemical components of the CNFs before and after loading CDs were also analyzed by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopies (XPS, Figure S7), and the results display that there is no significant difference for CNFs and CNFs@CDs (Fig. 3h). Accordingly, the combination of CDs with CNFs does not induce the variation of the chemical structures. Nevertheless, the optical absorption in the entire visible region is enhanced by CDs (Fig. 3i), suggesting higher sunlight harvesting and utilization. It should be noted that the CDs make CNFs turn to black color under hydrothermal treatment. In the absence of CDs, CNFs can’t change color (Figure S8) though they suffer from the same processing procedure with those of CNFs@CDs.

Apart from the hydration effects for water activation, the oxygen-related functional groups could promote organic contamination adsorption by forming hydrogen bonds [27,29]. To this end, the methylene blue (MB) as a model pollutant dye was demonstrated whether the CNFs@CDs had adsorption ability to dyes or not. At a given concentration of MB, the CNFs@CDs exhibits a remarkably higher adsorption capacity than pure CNFs under dark condition (Fig. 4
a, Figure S9). This could be attributed to much more adsorption sites and broader pore size distributions due to the introduction of CDs [23]. Most importantly, solar irradiation can further enhance the adsorption capacity of CNFs@CDs for MB, whereas this case cannot take place on pure CNFs (Fig. 4 b). The increase of MB adsorption capacity for CNFs@CDs is probably due to the localization gradients of concentration and temperature induced by solar water evaporation [30]. For CNFs@CDs, the rapid water evaporation is able to induce a localized concentration increase of MB at the evaporating interface, providing more opportunities of MB molecules for contacting with active sites. On the other hand, the photothermal conversion through CDs endows a localized temperature gradient upon them, affecting the adsorption process of MB based on the adsorption thermodynamic theory. Since commonly adsorption is an endothermic process [30], the higher temperature near the surface of CDs favors the MB uptake on them, resulting in the enhanced adsorption capacity of CNFs@CDs by solar irradiation. To confirm it, we also investigated the effects of the initial concentrations of MB on adsorption behaviors. It is clear that the adsorption masses of CNFs@CDs for MB are determined by the concentrations of MB (Fig. 4 c). Furthermore, the removal rates of MB in the solution with different concentrations were examined under the same conditions of solution volume and illumination intensity. Interestingly, Fig. 4 d displays that the removal ability of CNFs@CDs towards MB is independent with its concentrations in solution. Meanwhile, the evaporation performance of CNFs@CDs after adsorbing MB is unchanged (Figure S10), implying the superior adaptability of our presented evaporator for wastewater treatment. What’s more, the adsorbed dyes in CNFs@CDs can be recycled by soaking in the ethanol solution (Figure S11).

The solar desalination performance of CNFs@CDs was evaluated in synthetic seawater samples with different salinities ranging from 0 to 20 wt%. Fig. 5
a shows the salt-blocking issue of the solar evaporator of CNFs@CDs in a highly concentrated brine solution during continuous desalination operation under 1 sun irradiation. The surface of the CNFs@CDs covered by a layer of precipitated salt can be clearly observed (Figure S12). This layer of salts will resist the sunlight absorption of the CNFs@CDs underneath due to the opaque white color of the salt layer and the high sunlight reflectance [9,31–35]. To prevent the salt accumulation on the surface of evaporator, we designed channel arrays by simply drilling in CNFs@CDs. In consideration of the cooperation between the steam-generation efficiency and antifouling property, the diameter and space-distance of the drilled pores were optimized firstly and the corresponding optimal sizes were 0.5 and 3.0 mm, respectively (Figure S13 and Table S2). As shown in Fig. 5 b, the ER of the evaporator with millimeter-scale drilled channels is stable while that of the evaporator without drilling reduces gradually during continuous working for 6 h with one sun irradiation. This verifies that the drilled channel arrays are able to effectively inhibit salt formation on the surface of CNFs@CDs (Figure S14). From Fig. 5 c, the equilibrium temperature of the plateau region (resulted from the tremendous energy consumption of evaporation[36]) rises again because of salt formation at the surface. When channel arrays are drilled in CNFs@CDs, this equilibrium temperature almost keeps unaltered during continuous solar irradiation. Most notably, the formed salt layer upon CNFs@CDs can be re-dissolved into bulk water through fluid convection between the evaporator and water reservoir after turning off the light source (Fig. 5 d and Figure S15). According to repeatedly switching experiments displayed in Fig. 5 d, the evaporator of CNFs@CDs possesses the excellent regenerated performance. Meanwhile, its evaporation performance of CNFs@CDs was not changed after soaking 14 days in brine as well (Figure S16).

Theoretically, the drilled channels in CNFs@CDs endow a higher water flux than its intrinsic channels and thus the salt concentration in the drilled channels will be remarkably lower than that in the intrinsic pores [9,37]. The resultant in-plane concentration gradients give rise to a spontaneous salt exchange between them [37], transferring concentrated brine in the drilled channels back to the bulk solution (Fig. 5 e). Clearly, the drilled channels make fluid convection accelerate and improve salt-rejection ability of solar interfacial desalination.

In view of the cost and scalability, we further assessed the cost-effectiveness (ε) of our presented evaporator according to the reported equation[27]: ε=ṁ/c (in which c is the materials cost, $•m−2). The materials cost of our presented evaporator was calculated as merely 13.622 $•m−2 (Table S3-S5). In combination of the ER value (ṁ, kg•m−2•h−1), the achieved cost-effectiveness is 183.5 g•h−1•$−1, which implies 183.5 g of purified water can be obtained in one hour as spend one U.S. dollar. As the data provided in Table S6, our solar evaporator outperforms the previously reported evaporation systems in terms of cost-effectiveness.

We have firstly demonstrated that the different biomass wastes can be used to prepare CNFs as carriers with a consistent property just by purely chemical treatment. Under hydrothermal conditions, CDs are grafted on the surface of as-prepared CNFs to act as photo-thermal agents and enable fast water evaporation with ER of 2.5 kg m−2h−1 and about 96.45% solar-to-vapor efficiency under one sun irradiation, which also outperforms mostly reported evaporation systems regarding the cost-effectiveness ($13.622 m−2 of total materials cost). The obtained CNFs@CDs evaporator not only allows good hydration ability to lower the evaporation enthalpy, but can also remove dye contaminants effectively through adsorption reaction. Meanwhile, the CNFs@CDs can realize long-term antifouling solar desalination by means of rationally drilled millimeter-sized channels to improve the horizontal and spontaneous exchange of salts between the drilled channels and the intrinsic channels in CNFs@CDs. Given the sustainable biomass resources and scalable fabrication process, this work offers a promising strategy towards construct low-cost evaporators with the excellent water purification performance.

Yifan Wang: Data curation, Writing – original draft, Investigation. Qing Chang: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis. Chaorui Xue: Investigation, Visualization. Jinlong Yang: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Shengliang Hu: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province (20210302123037), China; Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province, China; Program for the Innovative Talents of Higher Education Institutions of Shanxi, China; Key Research and Development Plan (International Cooperation) of Shanxi Province (201903D421082), China; Transformation of Scientific and Technological Achievements Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (TSTAP), China. We also thank our collaborator Dr. Adrian Trinchi of the CSIRO Australia for the discussion on this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2022.04.061.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
